sourcematerial: Using species distribution models, Bellard et al. [7] found that, on average, insects belonging to the IUCN’s list “100 of the world’s worst invasive alien species” would increase their suitable range by 18% in 2050. Similarly, invasive alien hematophagous arthropods will likely do so in the future [28, 29].; Recent assessments estimated that the economic cost of invasive alien species reached at least US$ 2.168 trillion over the last 4 decades (see Living Figure in [22], and https:// boris leroy. com/ invac ost/ invac ost_ livin gfigu re. html), with Ae d e s mosquitoes being the most expensive genus among aquatic and semi-aquatic invaders, with a total cost of potentially US$ 311 billion [23].

More Notes: Cost assessed over the last 4 decades ending in 2023.

Using species distribution models, Bellard et al. [7] found that, on average, insects belonging to the IUCN’s list “100 of the world’s worst invasive alien species” would increase their suitable range by 18% in 2050. Similarly, invasive alien hematophagous arthropods will likely do so in the future [28, 29].; Recent assessments estimated that the economic cost of invasive alien species reached at least US$ 2.168 trillion over the last 4 decades (see Living Figure in [22], and https:// boris leroy. com/ invac ost/ invac ost_ livin gfigu re. html), with Ae d e s mosquitoes being the most expensive genus among aquatic and semi-aquatic invaders, with a total cost of potentially US$ 311 billion [23].

More Notes: Additional cost due to 55 year management delays for Aedes genus. Assuming the cost occurred with a 55-year delay from 2023 (paper publication year).
sourcematerial: Rapid response to eradicate invaders post-invasion as well as the implementation of effective biosecurity control pre-invasion (i.e. biosurveillance and intensive monitoring) are up to ten times cheaper for mosquito-borne diseases than waiting and paying for accrued damages. A recent modeling study using logistic response curves to resemble impact dynamics showed that for the Aedes genus, current management delays of 55 years have led to an additional cost of US$ 4.57 billion that could have been avoided, showing the crucial importance of acting early against future invasions and other global changes. With climate change projections over the next 3 decades likely to increase the area occupied by current arthropod invaders by 18%, the need for better biosecurity control to mitigate hematophagous arthropod nuisance biting and disease has never been stronger. Page 11, Cuthbert et al. Parasites & Vectors (2023) 16:291.

More Notes: Projected increase in area occupied by arthropod invaders over the next 3 decades, starting from 2023 (publication year).
